lesson:
見方みかた is literally 見み (see) + 方かた (way, method) — your way of seeing. Not what you see, but how you see it. 見方みかたを変かえる (change your perspective) is one of those phrases that shows up in every self-help book in Japan, but it's also perfectly natural in daily conversation: 見方みかたによっては正ただしい (depending on how you look at it, it's correct).

Watch the pitch: みかた with this kanji means "viewpoint." But there's a homophone — 味方みかた, also みかた, meaning "ally" or "someone on your side." Context always makes it obvious: if you're talking about opinions, it's 見方みかた. If you're talking about people who've got your back, it's 味方みかた. In writing the kanji settles it instantly, but in speech you might get a split second of ambiguity that resolves as the sentence unfolds.

The 〜方かた pattern is productive. 食たべ方かた (way of eating), 使つかい方かた (way of using), 考かんがえ方かた (way of thinking). Once you recognize it, 見方みかた stops being a vocabulary word and becomes a predictable compound.

The kanji 鳥とり is a picture of a bird. Not metaphorically — literally. The oracle bone form was a bird in profile: head, beak, body, tail feathers, legs. Over 3,000 years of simplification turned it into the modern character, but you can still trace the silhouette if you squint. The four dots at the bottom are the tail feathers. The horizontal stroke near the top is the eye.

There's a related kanji you should know: 鳥とり vs 烏からす (crow). Notice that 烏 is missing the horizontal stroke that represents the eye. The folk explanation: crows are so black you can't see their eyes. Whether or not that's the real etymology, it's impossible to forget once you've heard it.

とり in everyday Japanese does double duty. It means "bird" generically, but in the kitchen it means "chicken" — 鳥肉とりにく (chicken meat), or just 鳥とり at a yakitori stand. A restaurant menu saying 鳥とり isn't offering sparrow.

犬いぬ is 大だい (big, a person with arms spread) with a dot. That dot is the dog — or more precisely, it turns the person into a four-legged animal. The oldest forms showed a complete dog in profile, but as the character simplified over millennia, it collapsed into "person-shape plus one distinguishing mark." The dot is all that separates dog from big. Miss it when writing and you've got a completely different character.

This kanji shows up as a radical in dozens of animal and beast-related characters: 狼おおかみ (wolf), 猫ねこ (cat), 猿さる (monkey), 獣けもの (beast). When you see 犭 (the squished version of 犬いぬ) on the left side of a kanji, expect an animal.

The word いぬ is emotionally neutral in a way that might surprise you. Japanese has warmer alternatives: ワンちゃん is the baby-talk/affectionate version (from the onomatopoeia ワンワン, a dog's bark in Japanese). Calling someone's beloved pet 犬いぬ to their face isn't rude, but ワンちゃん shows you care.

返へん (return) + 事じ (thing, matter). A "returned thing" — you got a question, now you're sending something back. Simple etymology, but the word carries more social weight than English "reply" does.

In Japanese households and classrooms, 返事へんじ specifically means answering when called. A parent calls your name — 返事へんじは？ means "where's your answer?" i.e., "say something when I call you." The expected response is はい. Not answering when spoken to — giving no 返事へんじ — is a distinct kind of rudeness in Japan, especially from children. 返事へんじをしなさい (answer me!) is something every Japanese kid has heard.

It's a する-noun: 返事へんじする means "to reply." メールの返事へんじがまだ来こない (the reply to my email still hasn't come) uses it as a plain noun. For written or more formal contexts, 返答へんとう or 回答かいとう exist, but 返事へんじ is the one you'll reach for every day.

また is the word Japanese uses to say goodbye without saying goodbye. またね — "again, right?" — means "see you." It promises a next time without specifying when. また明日あした (see you tomorrow). また来週らいしゅう (see you next week). The word faces forward: we'll do this again.

As an adverb, it simply means "again": また失敗しっぱいした (I failed again). また雨あめだ (rain again). But また also works as a conjunction meaning "also" or "and" in more formal writing: 彼かれは医者いしゃであり、また作家さっかでもある (he is a doctor and also a writer). This usage is stiffer than the conversational "again" sense.

No kanji in common use. The kanji 又また exists but you'll almost never see また written that way in modern text. It stays in hiragana, which gives it a soft, casual feel — fitting for a word that's fundamentally friendly.

meaning: period of 1 year, per year, annual
lesson:
年ねん (year) + 間かん (interval, span). Where 年とし alone is just "year," 年間ねんかん emphasizes the duration — the full stretch of time from start to finish. 三年間さんねんかん (a period of three years) vs 三年さんねん (three years). The difference is subtle: 三さん年とし can mean "year three" or just "three years," while 三さん年とし間ま unambiguously means "for the span of three years."

You'll see this 〜間ま suffix on other time words: 時間じかん (time/hours), 週間しゅうかん (weeks), 期間きかん (period). It always adds that sense of measured duration — you're counting how long something stretches, not just labeling a point in time.

年間売上ねんかんうりあげ (annual sales). 年間ねんかんパスポート (annual pass). When 年間ねんかん modifies a noun directly, it means "yearly" or "annual" — the entire year treated as a single unit.

青あお is the color that refuses to pick a lane. It covers what English calls blue and what English calls green, depending on context. The sky is 青あおい. The sea is 青あおい. But so is the "go" light at an intersection — 青信号あおしんごう (green light) — even though it's objectively green. Unripe fruit? 青あおい. A pale, sickly face? 顔かおが青あおい. Young and inexperienced? 青あおい.

This isn't a quirk of Japanese. Ancient languages worldwide tended to group blue and green under one term. Japanese eventually added 緑みどり for green, but 青あお never gave up its territory. Traffic lights, vegetables, lush mountains — anything that was 青あお before 緑みどり existed stayed 青あお. The word carries its history in its usage.

The kanji 青あお originally depicted a plant (生せい, life/growth) emerging with a vivid color — the intense blue-green of new growth. That root meaning of "vivid, fresh" is why 青あお extends to youth (青春せいしゅん, youth — literally "blue spring") and inexperience (青二才あおにさい, greenhorn).

The kanji 赤あか is 大だい (big, a person) over 火ひ (fire). A person over flames — and what color do you see? The character is its own mnemonic. Some scholars trace it to the reddened skin of someone standing near fire, others to the color of the flames themselves. Either way: person + fire = red.

赤あか does more than name a color. 赤あかちゃん means "baby" — not because babies are red (though newborns are), but because 赤あか carries a connotation of "bare, raw, undisguised." A 赤あかちゃん is a naked little being with nothing to hide. 赤あかの他人たにん (a complete stranger) uses the same logic — someone with absolutely no connection to you. 赤あか intensifies: 赤字あかじ (red ink = financial deficit), just like English "in the red."

As an adjective, you'll use 赤あかい. As a noun — the color itself — it's just 赤あか. The two coexist: 赤あかい花はな (a red flower) vs 赤あかが好すき (I like red).

If you ask someone for directions in Japan, the answer will almost certainly involve this word. 次つぎの信号しんごうを右みぎに曲まがって (turn right at the next traffic light). 信号しんごう is the landmark that organizes Japanese navigation — intersections with traffic lights are the waypoints everything else is measured from.

The kanji: 信しん (trust) + 号ごう (sign, call). A trusted sign. The word technically covers any signal — radio signals, smoke signals — but in daily life it means traffic light, full stop. 信号しんごうが赤あかだ (the light is red). 信号しんごうを無視むしする (run a red light).

The green light is 青信号あおしんごう — a "blue signal," not 緑みどり信号しんごう. This is 青あお's territory. Yellow is 黄信号きしんごう. Red is 赤信号あかしんごう. And there's a proverb: 赤信号あかしんごう、みんなで渡わたれば怖こわくない (a red light isn't scary if everyone crosses together) — a darkly funny commentary on Japanese group mentality.

This is the second entry for 円えん in the list (it appeared earlier as a basic vocabulary item), and both meanings — "yen" and "circle" — trace back to the same root. The Japanese currency was named 円えん because the first modern coins were round, and 円えん means "round" or "circle." The character originally depicted something enclosed and complete — a perfect shape with no edges.

As currency: 百円ひゃくえん (100 yen), 千円せんえん (1,000 yen). No plural, no counter word needed — just number + 円えん. It's one of the simplest counters in the language. いくら？三百円さんびゃくえん。 (How much? 300 yen.)

The "circle" meaning lives mostly in compounds: 円形えんけい (circular shape), 円満えんまん (harmonious — a well-rounded relationship). By itself, まる or 丸まる is more common for "circle" in everyday speech. But knowing that 円えん means round gives 円満 and 円滑えんかつ (smooth) an intuitive feel: things that are round roll smoothly.

非ひ (non-, un-) + 常じょう (ordinary, normal) + に. "In a not-ordinary way." That etymology tells you everything about when to use it: 非常ひじょうに is formal, slightly stiff, and carries more weight than とても. A news anchor says 非常ひじょうに. A professor says 非常ひじょうに. Your friend at a bar says とても or めっちゃ.

非常ひじょうに難むずかしい問題もんだい (an extremely difficult problem). 非常ひじょうに重要じゅうようです (it is extremely important). The word pairs naturally with weighty adjectives and formal contexts. Using it to describe your lunch would sound comically stiff — like saying "exceedingly" in English to describe a sandwich.

非常ひじょう without the に works as a noun meaning "emergency": 非常口ひじょうぐち (emergency exit), 非常事態ひじょうじたい (state of emergency). Same kanji, same root idea — something beyond the ordinary — but one is an adverb of degree and the other is a noun of crisis.

複ふく (duplicate, multiple) + 雑ざつ (miscellaneous, mixed). Multiple things mixed together — complicated. The kanji practically define the word: when many different elements get tangled up, you've got 複雑ふくざつ.

This word describes situations, not puzzles. A math problem is 難むずかしい (difficult). A bureaucratic process with seventeen forms, three offices, and contradictory rules is 複雑ふくざつ. Human relationships are 複雑ふくざつ. 複雑ふくざつな気持きもち (complicated feelings) is something Japanese people say constantly — when you're happy and sad at the same time, when something good happened but it cost something, when you can't name a single clean emotion. It's one of the most emotionally honest phrases in the language.

As a な-adjective: 複雑ふくざつな問題もんだい (a complex problem). As a noun with する: 事態じたいが複雑化ふくざつかする (the situation becomes complicated) — the 化か suffix turns it into a verb of transformation.

Dictionaryふくざつだ
Negativeふくざつじゃない
Pastふくざつだった
Past negふくざつじゃなかった
Adverbふくざつに
て-formふくざつで

平へい (flat, level, calm) + 和 (harmony, Japan). A flat, harmonious state — peace. Both kanji carry enormous cultural weight in Japan. 平へい is in 平成へいせい (the Heisei era, 1989-2019). 和 is in 令和れいわ (the current era) and is one of the kanji used to refer to Japan itself (和食わしょく = Japanese food, 和室わしつ = Japanese-style room).

平和へいわ carries particular weight in postwar Japan. Hiroshima's Peace Memorial Park is 平和記念公園へいわきねんこうえん. Article 9 of the Japanese constitution — the clause renouncing war — is called the 平和条項へいわじょうこう. The word isn't just a dictionary entry. It's tied to a specific national wound and the resolve that followed.

In lighter usage: 平和へいわな一日いちにち (a peaceful day). 今日きょうは平和へいわだった (today was uneventful/peaceful). Young people sometimes use it sarcastically — 平和へいわだな〜 when nothing's happening, when life is boringly calm. The な-adjective usage is straightforward.

Dictionaryへいわだ
Negativeへいわじゃない
Pastへいわだった
Past negへいわじゃなかった
Adverbへいわに
て-formへいわで

meaning: to turn around, go around, rotate, revolve
lesson:
The kanji 回かい is a visual loop — a square spiral, something turning in on itself. The oldest forms showed a whirlpool. Rotation is baked into the character's shape.

回かいる covers an enormous range of circular motion. A top spins: コマが回まわる. The earth revolves: 地球ちきゅうが回まわる. You tour a city: 街まちを回まわる. Gossip gets around: 噂うわさが回まわる. Your head spins when you're dizzy: 頭あたまが回まわる. The connecting thread is always circular movement — something going around, making a circuit, completing a loop.

Pair: 回かいる (intransitive, things spin on their own) vs 回まわす (transitive, you spin something). ハンドルを回まわす (turn the steering wheel). 順番じゅんばんを回まわす (pass the turn along). The す ending is your signal that someone is doing the spinning.

Particles: As an intransitive motion verb, 回かいる takes を for the path or place you go around — 公園こうえんを回まわる (go around the park). This を marks the route, not a direct object. For "spin" uses, the spinning thing is the subject with が.

Passive に回まわられる: someone going around you, outflanking you — 敵てきに回まわられた (the enemy got around me). に marks who did the circling.

Causative を／に回まわらせる: making someone go around. を for coercive — 新人しんじんを回まわらせる (make the new hire do the rounds). に for permissive.

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 回す (まわす)

若わか (young) + 者もの (person). Transparent compound, but the word has a specific register: it's how older people and the media refer to young people as a group. News headlines love 若者わかもの: 若者わかものの投票率とうひょうりつ (youth voter turnout), 若者わかもの離ばなれ (young people drifting away from something). That last pattern — 若わか者の〇〇離れ — is a perennial Japanese media cliche. Young people leaving behind beer, cars, TV, marriage. Every generation gets this headline.

A young person wouldn't normally call themselves 若者わかもの. It's a third-person label, slightly clinical, the way "youth" sounds in English. You'd say 若わかい人ひと in casual conversation, or just refer to specific age groups. 若者わかもの is the word you hear in discussions about young people, not from them.

The 者しゃ suffix is productive: 医者いしゃ (doctor), 学者がくしゃ (scholar), 記者きしゃ (reporter). It always marks "a person who is or does [X]." Here, a person who is young.

The kanji 雪ゆき has 雨あめ (rain) on top — the radical for all weather that falls from the sky (雲くも cloud, 雷 thunder, 霧 fog). Below it, ヨ, which in this character represented a hand sweeping — the idea of wiping clean. Snow covers and blankets everything, erasing the landscape. Rain that cleans the world white.

Japan has a deep relationship with snow. The country's western coast — the 日本海にほんかい side — gets buried under some of the heaviest snowfall on Earth. 雪国ゆきぐに (snow country) is both a geographic term for these regions and the title of Kawabata's famous novel. 雪祭ゆきまつり (snow festival) — Sapporo's annual event — draws millions.

The sound ゆき is soft, almost whispered — yu-ki, two gentle syllables. It happens to sound nothing like "snow" in English, but if you try to say it quietly, it has the hushed quality of watching snowfall. A useful coincidence for anchoring the sound to the meaning.
